one_line_takeaway: Compromising just one entry robot in an LLM-coordinated multi-robot fleet lets adversarial intent hitchhike on legitimate inter-robot messages to jailbreak the rest of the team -- and this propagation persists even when individual robots (including GPT-5.1) show perfect single-turn refusal robustness.
key_finding: Obedience reaches 1.00 in the strongest observed case (Kimi-K2, Formation Escort) and infectiousness rises to 0.90 (Gemini-2.5-Flash, Warehouse Patrol), with the attack requiring as few as 3.0 rounds (Kimi-K2, Formation Escort) to compromise all robots while maintaining a stealthiness score of 0.81. Critically, high per-robot refusal robustness does NOT prevent system-level compromise: in Warehouse Patrol, GPT-3.5-Turbo attains a perfect Ssecurity of 100.0 yet still yields Cinf of 0.69, while GPT-5.1 (frontier tier) attains a perfect Ssecurity of 100.0 yet still yields Cinf of 0.62 and Oobedience of 0.77 -- i.e., strong single-agent jailbreak resistance does not translate into resistance to propagation via peer coordination messages. Event-level analysis (Table 2) shows that of 832 total unsafe events, 61.5% (Efwd) were triggered by forwarded/relayed messages rather than direct attacker interaction with Robot 0 (only 38.5%, ER0), and 44.2% of runs exhibited unsafe events at three or more propagation hops, with 10.3% reaching at least five hops -- indicating that message-mediated multi-hop spread, not isolated entry-robot deviation, is the dominant risk driver. Increasing model capability (GPT-3.5-Turbo to GPT-4o to GPT-5.1) reduced rounds-to-compromise and steps but did not eliminate propagation; deeper multi-hop chains were actually more prevalent in the frontier model per Figure 4, suggesting stronger reasoning does not inherently suppress cascade dynamics.
core_contribution: The paper introduces InfectBot, a black-box attack paradigm and staged propagation algorithm (Algorithm 1) against LLM-controlled multi-robot collaboration systems. The attacker interacts with only a single 'entry' robot (Robot 0) via natural-language jailbreak prompts; the compromised robot then relays a transferable malicious payload ('Protocol-Unity-Gamma' style prompts) to peer robots through normal inter-robot coordination messages, causing the adversarial intent to cascade to the rest of the fleet without the attacker ever contacting those other robots directly. The paper also proposes three normalized propagation metrics -- Obedience O(tau), Infectiousness C_inf(tau), and Stealthiness S_stealth(tau) -- plus two auxiliary indicators, propagation depth D(tau) and time-to-compromise R(tau), and builds a ROS 2 Humble / NVIDIA Isaac Sim 4.5.0 + Isaac Lab 2.1 multi-robot simulation environment (with real Unitree Go2 hardware experiments via the official ROS 2/SDK2 stack) to validate the attack across three scenarios: warehouse patrol (dereliction of duty), hospital (privacy compromise via illicit camera activation), and formation escort (public safety hazard, e.g. steering a hazardous-cargo-carrying robot into a crowd).
threat_model: Black-box setting: the attacker has no access to model parameters, gradients, or internal reasoning of any robot, and can infer vulnerabilities only from observed responses/actions. The attacker is restricted to natural-language interaction with a single designated entry robot (Robot 0); all other robots are only reachable indirectly through the multi-robot system's own inter-robot communication channel. The attacker's goal is to trigger cluster-wide unsafe/unauthorized physical behavior (dereliction of duty, privacy violation, public-safety hazard) by having the entry robot itself relay and propagate the malicious protocol to peers.
limitations: Evaluation relies on the authors' own hand-defined metrics (Obedience, Infectiousness, Stealthiness) with no established prior benchmark for coordinated multi-robot security, and the paper explicitly notes 'no established security benchmark exists for coordinated robot systems,' so it adapts the single-agent BADROBOT dataset as a proxy rather than testing against a purpose-built multi-robot defense baseline. No dedicated multi-robot defense mechanism is proposed or evaluated -- only a lightweight YAML system-prompt guardrail is used as a baseline, and the authors state 'available defense baselines for coordinated robot settings are limited.' Physical/hardware validation is only briefly mentioned (Unitree Go2 ROS 2/SDK2 integration) without a dedicated quantitative results table for hardware runs distinct from the Isaac Sim results; the reported quantitative tables (1-3) appear to be simulation-based. The three evaluated scenarios (warehouse, hospital, formation escort) and five target LLMs, while spanning deployment tiers, remain a limited sample relative to the diversity of real multi-robot architectures and coordination protocols (the authors note plans to study alternative communication/coordination architectures, e.g. centralized planning, in future work).

datasets_used: BADROBOT jailbreak dataset (adapted from Zhang et al., 2025a) as the source of standardized jailbreak variants (Bcj, Bsm, Bcd) and an 'In-the-Wild' jailbreak prompt set used for the system-prompt robustness/ERR evaluation
Authors' own three custom multi-robot simulation scenarios built in NVIDIA Isaac Sim 4.5.0 / Isaac Lab 2.1 over ROS 2 Humble: Warehouse Patrol, Hospital Privacy, Formation Escort
quantitative_results: Time-to-compromise as low as 3.0 rounds (Kimi-K2, Formation Escort) with Stealthiness (Sstealth) 0.81 in that setting
GPT-3.5-Turbo Warehouse Patrol: Ssecurity=100.00 yet Cinf=0.69
Gemini-2.5-Flash Warehouse Patrol: Cinf=0.90 despite Ssecurity=65.30
GPT-5.1 (frontier reference, warehouse task): Ssecurity=100.00, Scapability=100.00, Scompliance=100.00, yet Cinf=0.62 and Oobedience(Oobd)=0.77, with rounds R=4.40 and steps S=10.20
GPT-4o (Go2-Edu tier, warehouse task): Ssecurity=91.20, Cinf=0.76, Oobd=0.74, R=5.80 rounds
Unsafe-event source breakdown (Table 2, 832 total events): 38.5% directly triggered by Robot 0 (ER0=320), 61.5% forwarded/infectious (Efwd=512)
Propagation depth breakdown: 44.2% of events at >=3 hops (368), 27.2% at >=4 hops (226), 10.3% at >=5 hops (86)
System-prompt utility/security scores (Table 1) span 74.96 to 95.33 across models/tasks; Gemini-2.5-Flash attains 95.33 in Hospital Privacy, GPT-3.5-Turbo reaches 93.40 in Warehouse Patrol
baselines_compared: Three systematic jailbreak variants Bcj, Bsm, Bcd (from BADROBOT-derived set)
In-the-Wild jailbreak prompt set (from Zhang et al., 2025a BADROBOT)
Target LLMs compared across deployment tiers: GPT-3.5-Turbo, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Kimi-K2 (mainstream/voice-interface tier); GPT-4o (developer/Go2-Edu tier); GPT-5.1 (frontier reference tier)
